body: SCAD is chronically under-recognised. We now have a SCAD pathway triggered when any woman <55 without traditional risk factors presents with ACS. Key: avoid thrombolytics. Dual antiplatelet for 12 months — some centres extend to 24 in recurrent SCAD or multivessel disease.

Great case. The Type 2 imaging pattern (intramural haematoma without intimal tear) is actually the most common SCAD subtype — often missed on routine angio. OCT or IVUS is game-changing for confirming the diagnosis.
Did you screen for connective tissue disease? Fibromuscular dysplasia co-exists in ~50-86% of SCAD cases. Renal artery FMD screening by CTA abdomen is our routine now.

On anti-platelet duration — the SCAD-1 pilot RCT (n=80) is underway but results not expected until 2027. Most expert consensus suggests 12 months DAPT, then aspirin indefinitely in recurrent presenters. Beta-blockers preferred over CCBs per most guidance.

Important note: SCAD incidence spikes peripartum — particularly in the first few weeks postpartum. Worth flagging in obstetric units. 1 in 1,000 spontaneous SCDs in peripartum women in published series.

Plasma p-tau217 (Lumipulse or Lilly assay) is now showing AUC >0.95 for differentiating AD from non-AD dementias in research cohorts. We are running a pilot to use it as a gatekeeper before amyloid PET referral. Reduces PET cost burden significantly.

Good discussion. For those who can access CSF, Lumipulse Abeta42/40 ratio + p-tau181 has ~90% concordance with amyloid PET in our small internal series. Cost: ~₹18,000 vs ₹2.4L for PET. Worth validating locally.

Metabolic comorbidities matter here. Insulin resistance (type 2 DM, obesity) significantly accelerates amyloid accumulation. I am increasingly convinced metabolic management is an underutilised preventive lever — GLP-1 receptor agonist trials in AD are intriguing.

The neuropsychiatric symptom burden (anxiety, agitation, depression) in early AD is often undertreated while awaiting disease-modifying therapy access. Citalopram for agitation, mirtazapine for sleep/appetite in early MCI — do not wait for the expensive drugs to manage QoL.

FDG-PET brain has relatively good sensitivity for AD pattern vs frontotemporal dementia differentiation and is more accessible than amyloid PET. For resource-constrained settings, FDG-PET + clinical + structural MRI (hippocampal volumes) can be a reasonable workup.

Combining esketamine with psychodynamic therapy in 11 patients over the last year. Anecdotally the response duration does seem longer with concurrent therapy vs drug alone — but n is too small for any conclusions. Anyone aware of an ongoing RCT?

The REMS programme overhead is genuinely prohibitive in smaller centres. We had to designate a dedicated REMS coordinator (an overworked nurse practitioner). The 2-hour monitoring window per dose adds ~15 hours of nursing time per patient per month. A simplified monitoring pathway for long-term stable patients is needed.

For patients who cannot afford esketamine, oral ketamine (off-label, compounded) has some RCT data — ELEKT-D trial (2023) showed 0.5 mg/kg sublingual 3x/week for 4 weeks: response rate ~20% better than placebo at week 4. Not regulatory-approved but manageable at home without REMS burden.

Hepatotoxicity is worth monitoring with repeated esketamine use. We check LFTs at baseline and every 3 months. One patient with pre-existing MASH had transaminase elevation — reduced frequency to monthly and LFTs normalised.

Drug interaction point: esketamine + MAOIs is absolutely contraindicated. Serotonin syndrome risk with concurrent strong serotonergic agents. In patients on SNRIs, watch carefully in the 2-hour observation window for tachycardia and agitation — these are early serotonin syndrome flags, not just ketamine side effects.

ROX index is genuinely useful but has limitations in obese patients and those with chronic hypercapnia. We now use ROX + serial ABGs (PaO2/FiO2 ratio trend) together. A rising PaCO2 on serial ABG at 1h is our strongest trigger to escalate to NIV despite a reassuring ROX.

Heated humidification is essential for HFNC — unhumidified high-flow causes mucosal drying and ciliary dysfunction within 4 hours, paradoxically worsening secretion clearance. Surprising how many centres are still using cold, dry HFNC.

In our ED we found HFNC failed more often in patients with resting RR >34 at initiation — these patients seem to have crossed a fatigue threshold where the physiology of HFNC cannot compensate. We still try, but earlier ICU discussion for RR >34 + SpO2 <92% despite 60L HFNC.

Infection control point: HFNC generates aerosol. Earlier COVID data suggested 5-fold increase in viral load dispersal vs standard oxygen. Negative-pressure rooms or at least surgical-grade ventilated isolation bays are important for suspected/confirmed infectious aetiologies.

In our oncology HDU, immunosuppressed patients on HFNC have worse outcomes than general ICU patients with similar ROX scores. The immunosuppression masks respiratory drive — patients tolerate worsening hypoxia without perceived dyspnoea. Earlier intubation threshold in neutropenic fever on HFNC.

For exercise restriction, the ESC 2023 guidelines recommend return to competitive sport at 3-6 months with repeat CMR showing no active inflammation (T1/T2 normalisation). Recreational exercise can resume earlier. We use a graded exercise test at 3 months — if no symptoms/arrhythmias on stress echo, allow moderate-intensity activity.

Not cardiology, but increasingly seeing post-COVID myocarditis clearance letters requested before sports physicals in young athletes. Worth developing a standardised return-to-sport certification pathway at your centre.

Paediatric post-COVID myocarditis is our concern here. MIS-C overlap spectrum — we are seeing more adolescents with LGE on CMR at 6-month follow-up after initial MIS-C, even those who appeared to recover fully. Routine CMR at 6 months is our current policy for MIS-C.

On de novo AF: we have identified 3 cases of new-onset AF at 3-6 months post-COVID in the 30-45 age group — all had elevated inflammatory markers (CRP >10) at COVID presentation. Worth prospective tracking.

Colchicine 0.5 mg BD was well-tolerated in our post-COVID myopericarditis patients. Would not omit it in favour of NSAIDs alone — the anti-inflammatory mechanism is distinct and complementary. Duration: 3 months minimum.

The real value of RUSH is in separating hypovolaemic from cardiogenic shock early. We had a case of apparent septic shock in hypertension — RUSH showed a grossly dilated RV with D-sign and pericardial effusion. Actual diagnosis: massive PE with obstructive shock. Beta-agonists and fluids would have been catastrophic.

The BLUE (bedside lung ultrasound in emergency) protocol integrated into RUSH significantly improves respiratory component assessment. The A+B lines protocol is learnable in <3 hours. I trained our ID fellows on it after noticing ARDS vs HCAP was being confused in complex sepsis.

VTI (velocity-time integral) as a surrogate for fluid responsiveness is a valuable POCUS add-on. LVOT VTI <18 cm and variation >10% with PLR predicts fluid responsiveness with ~80% accuracy. Has essentially replaced our blind saline challenges.

Caution: POCUS quality varies massively with practitioner experience. In our ICU, a structured competency-based sign-off system (FUSIC in the UK; iSCUS equivalents) reduced POCUS misinterpretation errors significantly. Self-proclaimed POCUS competence without formal accreditation is a real patient safety issue.

IVC collapsibility index is the most debated POCUS parameter. In spontaneously breathing patients it is more reliable for fluid status. In ventilated patients with low PEEP, use with caution — PEEP itself affects IVC morphology. We use IVC only in conjunction with other RUSH components now, not in isolation.

Strong evidence here. Every antibiotic stewardship programme should include penicillin de-labelling. The downstream effects — including reducing 3rd-generation cephalosporin and fluoroquinolone use — are directly measurable on your antibiogram within 12-18 months.

We piloted rapid allergy de-labelling in our pre-anaesthesia assessment clinic. Graded oral amoxicillin challenge (25mg → 250mg → 500mg at 30-min intervals under observation) takes 2 hours and has an excellent safety profile. 0 anaphylaxis events in 180 challenges in our series.

In the ED, penicillin-labelled patients have consistently poorer outcomes for infective endocarditis (forced to use vancomycin vs amoxicillin-gentamicin), group A strep sepsis, and syphilis. The mortality signal from incorrect allergy labelling is actually quite clear in large retrospective analyses.

Co-amoxiclav is vastly superior to cephalosporins for clean orthopaedic surgical prophylaxis. Our infection rates in hip arthroplasty dropped from 1.4% → 0.7% when we moved allergic-labelled patients through a pre-op de-labelling pathway. The clinical value is not abstract — it is directly measurable.

Important distinction: penicillin skin testing has a negative predictive value >97% in large series. The old graded-challenge-only approach can skip skin testing for low-risk patients (remote history, mild reaction, no systemic features) — this significantly reduces clinic time without compromising safety.

Driving pressure monitoring is critical. In our NICU, even with "standard" low tidal volumes, patients with small compliant lungs can have driving pressures >18 cmH2O leading to injurious ventilation. Closed-loop ventilation (ASV/INTELLiVENT-ASV) automatically titrates to driving pressure targets — worth evaluating.

The resistance from cardiac surgery anaesthesia is real in our institution too. Their concern: low TV increases risk of atelectasis in single ventricle/post-CPB lungs with intrinsically high shunt. A middle ground — 6-8 mL/kg with PEEP titration and recruitment manoeuvre post-CPB — is our current cardiac surgical ICU protocol.

Mechanical power (a composite of TV, rate, PEEP, driving pressure) is emerging as the most comprehensive single ventilatory injury parameter. Target <17 J/min in non-ARDS, <12 J/min in ARDS. We have added mechanical power calculation to our daily ventilator rounds.

In our infectious disease-complicated respiratory failure patients (PCP pneumonia, severe influenza), I have noticed the lung mechanics change rapidly within 24-48 hours — compliance drops precipitously. Daily plateau pressure and driving pressure checks are essential, not just admission assessment.

Pre-oxygenation strategy matters enormously for downstream lung-protective ventilation success. High-flow + NIV in the ED during RSI, followed by immediate 6 mL/kg PBW at intubation, dramatically reduces the "first 30 minutes" lung injury window that often comes from post-intubation full-volume breaths.

The single most impactful intervention in our ICU was removing verbal orders entirely for high-alert medications (insulin, heparin, vasopressors, concentrated electrolytes). Every order must be entered in CPOE. Compliance took 6 months to embed culturally but near-eliminated this category of error.

Smart infusion pumps with a drug library (hard and soft limits) are genuinely life-saving for ICU infusions. Our analysis: 6.2% of programmed infusion orders triggered a soft limit alert, and 0.8% triggered a hard stop. Without the pump library, those 0.8% would have reached the patient.

Pharmacist presence at morning ICU rounds reduced medication errors by 66% in a Mayo Clinic trial. We now have a dedicated clinical pharmacist on daily ICU rounds. The cultural shift was harder than the logistics — intensivists had to genuinely accept pharmacist input as equals on the team.

Night-shift fatigue is the most under-addressed systems issue. Study after study shows error rates peak between 2-6am. A no-dose-calculation approach (using pre-printed weight-based dosing charts laminated in each bay) reduced night-shift arithmetic errors in our ED by 40%.

Antibiotic dosing errors in ICU are particularly dangerous. Renally adjusted antibiotic dosing is wrong in ~30% of prescriptions in published audits when done without pharmacist input. We use AUC-guided vancomycin dosing (not trough alone) with pharmacokinetic software — eliminated vancomycin toxicity in our unit.
